risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A list was produced by the Nottingham Clinical Trials Unit to allow simple (unrestricted) randomization" and "participants had completed the screening questions... they were given a study ID, allocated through the website... If participants consented, they were randomized by the portal designers at ANU." Comment: The paper explicitly reports a randomization list prepared by a Clinical Trials Unit for simple unrestricted randomization, and allocation was implemented through the website. This clearly indicates a proper random sequence was used.

Quote: "The trial (no. ISRCTN24529487; http://www.controlled-trials.com/) was designed to be administered mainly online. The MoodGYM developers were commissioned by the study investigators to construct a research portal that could allocate login identities, screen, take consent, randomize and direct participants to the appropriate arm of the study." Comment: Allocation was handled by an online research portal that enrolled, consented, and randomized participants, which indicates the recruiter did not have access to or foreknowledge of the next assignment; this is a mechanism that prevents allocation prediction. Quote: "In this way the randomization status of participants was concealed from their employers and from the research team until the study was completed." Comment: Although this explicitly states concealment from employers and the research team after randomization, combined with the portal-based automated allocation it supports low risk for allocation concealment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The study design aimed to be double-blind. Participants were told that they were participating in a trial of ‘online self-help’, comparing two approaches, and researcher bias was avoided completely because most data for the outcome measures were collected online. However, the telephone interviewers who recorded service use measures for economic analysis were not blind to the randomization status of the participants." Comment: Participants were not told which intervention was the experimental one and the main outcome data were collected online/self-report, making knowledge of allocation unlikely to have materially affected the dominant outcomes across the study. Although telephone interviewers were unblinded for service-use data, this appears secondary to the main self-reported outcomes and does not outweigh the overall low risk for this domain.

Quote: "The study design aimed to be double-blind. Participants were told that they were participating in a trial of ‘online self-help’, comparing two approaches, and researcher bias was avoided completely because most data for the outcome measures were collected online. However, the telephone interviewers who recorded service use measures for economic analysis were not blind to the randomization status of the participants." Comment: The main outcomes (WSAS, PHQ-9, CORE10, GAD, EQ-5D, self-assessed absence from work) were collected online by participant self-report, so the outcome assessor was effectively the participant. Because participants were told they were in a trial comparing two online self-help approaches and the paper states that most outcome data were collected online, there is no clear evidence that outcome measurement was influenced by assessor knowledge of allocation. The only explicitly unblinded assessors were telephone interviewers for service use/economic data, which are not the primary clinical outcomes here and are relatively objective record-based measures.

Quote: "The completion rate of the initial 637 participants was low... Completion was 359/637 (56%) at 6 weeks and 231/637 (36%) at 12 weeks." Comment: Attrition was very large overall, especially by 12 weeks, and the paper itself notes that "more participants were lost to follow-up from the MoodGYM arm than from the control arm, although this was not significant." Even with ITT/mixed modelling, the amount of missing outcome data is substantial enough that attrition could bias the result.
